what_it_does: A self-contained HTML reference page that lists and explains the Company's MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool surface: 64 tools grouped into 10 families, each described in plain language, plus a disambiguation of overloaded tool names.
observations: Static HTML file with embedded CSS, dark themed, responsive layout.
Enumerates 64 tools across 10 families: knowing, thinking, models, memory, live fabric, graph, reading state, marks, governance, right-hand-man.
Each tool has a monospace name and a plain-English description, often with allowed operations in parentheses.
Includes a 'name-traps' footnote clarifying overloaded terms like capability/capabilities, project, capture/ingest/corpus, mark/marks, flows/cascades, inbox/mailbox.
Claims the tool list is 'read live from the real schemas' despite being a static HTML document.
Heavy emphasis on operator gate, surfaced approvals, and self-description so users compose from real capabilities rather than invent them.
Contains navigation anchors for each family and CSS accent colors per section.
standouts: The central thesis that these tools are 'the right-hand-man's tools and yours, the same projected verb-space'.
Governance family where role edits, deletions, and new roles are surfaced for operator approval rather than self-applied.
Live fabric family with session-to-session messaging, session cloning from a past moment, voice rendering, and context compaction.
The explicit 'name-traps, disambiguated' section addressing overloaded vocabulary.
concerns: Static HTML is labeled as read from live schemas, but there is no visible mechanism or link proving live schema binding.
Descriptions are high-level and omit argument schemas, return shapes, and error behavior, so they cannot be used as API documentation alone.
Some tool names are very close to each other (e.g., flows vs list_cascades/run_cascade) and the distinction is only explained narratively.
No versioning or last-updated metadata, so drift from the actual MCP schema is possible.
notes: A human-readable catalog of the Company's MCP tool surface, organized by capability family and ending with a disambiguation glossary.
questions: Where are the underlying MCP schemas this page claims to be read from?
How does the operator-approval surface actually present itself to a user?
What is the precise difference between 'flows' and 'cascades' beyond 'code production-lines' vs 'declarative saved recipes'?
Is the 'Company' a layer on top of Claude Code, or a separate system, and how do their registries interact?
Are all 64 tools currently implemented, or are some aspirational?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to present every MCP tool in plain meaning, and the file does exactly that: it lists, groups, and glosses the tools without ranking them.
apparent_themes: Self-description and transparency of the tool surface.
Compositional thinking via roles, chains, panels, and graphs.
intention_signals: The file is trying to turn a potentially overwhelming tool schema into an operator-comprehensible verb-space, so a user can know what moves exist and compose from real capabilities rather than hallucinate them.
novelty: An interpretive, plain-meaning layer over a technical tool surface, plus an explicit 'name-traps' glossary that addresses overloaded terms rather than leaving them ambiguous.
contribution: A readable, grouped catalog and disambiguation of the MCP tool surface that a fused system would keep as operator-facing documentation to help users discover and correctly invoke tools.
summary_for_embedding: An HTML reference document listing 64 Company MCP tools in 10 families with plain-language descriptions and a name-disambiguation glossary.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a human-readable map of the available MCP tool surface so operators can compose from real capabilities without inventing tools.
